name: Palau

colors.symbolism: the transition from foreign rule to self-government and freedom
the full moon, traditionally regarded by Palauans as the optimum time for human activity such as fishing, planting, harvesting and ceremonies

description: A light-blue field bearing a yellow disc — representing the full moon — set slightly toward the hoist of the centre.
history: Adopted on 1 January 1981 when Palau achieved self-government as the Republic of Palau, then in free association with the United States; retained on full independence on 1 October 1994. The flag was the winning entry by John Blau Skebong in a national design competition. Despite the visual similarity to the flag of Japan, the symbolism is explicitly the full moon (not the sun), and the disc is offset toward the hoist rather than centred.

features.symbolism: the bright sky-blue field represents the transition from foreign domination to self-government — the colour was chosen to symbolise the freedom Palau achieved on becoming a self-governing entity, not the surrounding Pacific Ocean as is sometimes assumed

features.role: yellow disc representing the full moon
features.symbolism: the full moon, considered by Palauans the time of greatest tranquillity and best suited for human activity (fishing, planting, harvesting, festivals); also represents peace, love and tranquillity. The disc is positioned slightly toward the hoist of centre, suggesting the moon rather than (despite the visual similarity) the Japanese sun
